How We Build Engineering Culture at NovaTech

By Sarah Chen, VP of Engineering November 15, 2025 6 min read

At NovaTech, we believe that great engineering culture does not happen by accident. Over the past three years, we have grown from a team of 8 engineers to over 60, and along the way we have learned a lot about what makes a high-performing engineering organization.

Start with Psychological Safety

The foundation of our culture is psychological safety. We want every engineer to feel comfortable asking questions, admitting mistakes, and proposing bold ideas. We reinforce this through blameless post-mortems, open architecture decision records, and a no-interruption rule during technical presentations.

Invest in Developer Experience

We track our internal developer experience metrics quarterly. Our DX team works full-time on reducing build times, improving CI reliability, and maintaining internal tooling. Last quarter we reduced our average PR review cycle from 18 hours to under 4 hours by introducing automated code review suggestions and clearer ownership boundaries.

Learning as a First-Class Activity

Every engineer at NovaTech gets 10% time for learning. Some use it to take online courses, others contribute to open source, and some run internal workshops. We also host monthly tech talks where team members present on topics they are passionate about, from distributed systems to generative AI.

Collaboration Over Silos

We organize around cross-functional product squads rather than technology-specific teams. Each squad includes engineers, a product manager, and a designer. This structure keeps everyone close to the customer problem and reduces handoff friction.

Building a strong engineering culture is an ongoing effort, not a one-time project. We are constantly iterating on our processes based on feedback from the team. If this kind of environment sounds interesting to you, check out our careers page to see open positions.
